anchor.snippet: an algebraic variety is defined as the set of solutions of a system of polynomial equations
some definitions require an algebraic variety to be irreducible
Hilbert's Nullstellensatz provides a fundamental correspondence between ideals of polynomial rings and algebraic sets
an algebraic variety over a field is an integral (irreducible and reduced) scheme over that field
define the zero-locus Z ( S ) to be the set of points in A n on which the functions in S simultaneously vanish
A subset V of A n is called an affine algebraic set if V = Z ( S ) for some S
A nonempty affine algebraic set V is called irreducible if it cannot be written as the union of two proper algebraic subsets
An irreducible affine algebraic set is also called an affine variety
Affine varieties can be given a natural topology by declaring the closed sets to be precisely the affine algebraic sets
we define I ( V ) to be the ideal of all polynomial functions vanishing on V
the coordinate ring or structure ring of V is the quotient of the polynomial ring by this ideal
define the zero-locus of S to be the set of points in P n on which the functions in S vanish
A subset V of P n is called a projective algebraic set if V = Z ( S ) for some S
An irreducible projective algebraic set is called a projective variety
Projective varieties are also equipped with the Zariski topology by declaring all algebraic sets to be closed
let I ( V ) be the ideal generated by all homogeneous polynomials vanishing on V
A quasi-projective variety is a Zariski open subset of a projective variety
all varieties were by definition quasi-projective varieties
an abstract algebraic variety is usually defined to be an integral , separated scheme of finite type over an algebraically closed field

anchor.section: Existence of non-quasiprojective abstract algebraic varieties
anchor.snippet: One of the earliest examples of a non-quasiprojective algebraic variety were given by Nagata
A subvariety is a subset of a variety that is itself a variety
Hilbert's Nullstellensatz says that closed subvarieties of an affine or projective variety are in one-to-one correspondence
This is called a line in the affine plane
As g ( x , y ) is an absolutely irreducible polynomial, this is an algebraic variety
an algebraic curve that is not contained in any plane

annotations.label: General linear group as affine variety

anchor.snippet: It is an affine variety, since, in general, the complement of a hypersurface in an affine variety is affine
A finite product of it ( k × ) r is an algebraic torus , which is again an affine variety
A general linear group is an example of a linear algebraic group , an affine variety that has a structure of a group
the locus where gr M does not vanish is called the characteristic variety of M
A projective variety is a closed subvariety of a projective space
A plane projective curve is the zero locus of an irreducible homogeneous polynomial in three indeterminates
which defines a curve in P 2 called an elliptic curve
The Grassmannian variety G n ( V ) is the set of all n -dimensional subspaces of V
The Jacobian variety Jac( C ) of C is the kernel of this degree map
an abelian variety , a complete variety with a compatible abelian group structure on it

annotations.label: Elliptic curve is an abelian variety

anchor.snippet: in particular, an elliptic curve is an abelian variety

annotations.label: Moduli of curves of genus g

anchor.snippet: the set of isomorphism classes of smooth complete curves of genus g is called the moduli of curves of genus
a stable curve of genus g ≥ 2 , a not-necessarily-smooth complete curve
the set of isomorphism classes of stable curves of genus g ≥ 2 , is then a projective variety
The moduli of semistable vector bundles of a given rank n and a given degree d
Here X is an algebraic variety since it is a product of varieties
Another example of a non-affine non-projective variety is X = A 2 − (0, 0)
is not an algebraic variety (nor even an algebraic set)

annotations.label: Unitary group vs special linear group

anchor.snippet: a unitary group (over the complex numbers) is not an algebraic variety, while the special linear group SL n ( C ) is a closed subvariety

annotations.label: Variety iff coordinate ring is integral domain

anchor.snippet: An affine algebraic set V is a variety if and only if I ( V ) is a prime ideal
Every nonempty affine algebraic set may be written uniquely as a finite union of algebraic varieties

annotations.label: Product of varieties is a variety

anchor.snippet: A product of finitely many algebraic varieties (over an algebraically closed field) is an algebraic variety
We say V 1 and V 2 are isomorphic , and write V 1 ≅ V 2 , if there are regular maps
A scheme is a locally ringed space such that every point has a neighbourhood that, as a locally ringed space, is isomorphic to a spectrum of a ring
A complete variety is a variety such that any map from an open subset of a nonsingular curve into it can be extended uniquely to the whole curve
Every projective variety is complete, but not vice versa
An algebraic manifold is an algebraic variety that is also an m -dimensional manifold
algebraic manifolds are called Nash manifolds
The Riemann sphere is one example
